# Project Structure

When you run `kitz new my-app`, the CLI generates a complete, compiling project with a multi-panel layout, message types, and a test file. This page explains every file in the generated tree, how they connect, and how the marker comment system enables code generation.

---

## The Generated File Tree

```
my-app/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Cargo.toml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ .gitignore
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ src/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ main.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ app.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ messages.rs
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ panels/
‚îÇ       ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mod.rs
‚îÇ       ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ sidebar.rs
‚îÇ       ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ detail.rs
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ tests/
    ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ app_test.rs
```

The project is intentionally small. There are no macros, no build scripts, and no hidden configuration files. Every file is plain Rust that you can read, modify, and delete.

---

## File-by-File Breakdown

### `Cargo.toml`

```toml
[package]
name = "my-app"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[dependencies]
kitz = { version = "0.1", default-features = false }
ratatui = "0.30"
crossterm = "0.29"
color-eyre = "0.6"
```

Kitz is added with `default-features = false`. This excludes the CLI feature and its dependencies (clap, colored) from your application binary. You only need the framework library.

### `src/main.rs`

```rust
mod app;
mod messages;
mod panels;

use color_eyre::Result;

fn main() -> Result<()> {
    kitz::run(app::App::new())
}
```

The entry point declares three modules and calls `kitz::run()` with your application struct. The `run` function initializes the terminal, enters the event loop, and guarantees restoration on exit.

### `src/messages.rs`

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub enum Msg {
    SidebarNext,
    SidebarPrev,
    DetailUpdate(String),
    // kitz:messages
}
```

All application messages live in a single enum. This is the message type passed to `Application::update`. Every state transition in your app corresponds to one variant.

The `// kitz:messages` comment is a **marker** ‚Äî the CLI inserts new variants above it when you run `kitz generate`. More on markers below.

### `src/app.rs`

This is the central file. It contains the `App` struct and the `Application` trait implementation.

```rust
pub struct App {
    pub sidebar: panels::sidebar::SidebarPanel,
    pub detail: panels::detail::DetailPanel,
    // kitz:app-fields
    pub theme: kitz::theme::Theme,
}
```

Each panel is a field on the app struct. The `Application` trait implementation delegates rendering and key handling to these panel structs through match arms:

- `panels()` returns the layout.
- `panel_title(id)` matches on panel IDs to return display titles.
- `panel_view(id, ...)` matches on panel IDs to delegate rendering.
- `panel_key_hints(id)` matches on panel IDs to return key bindings.
- `panel_handle_key(id, key)` matches on panel IDs to delegate key input.
- `update(msg, ctx)` matches on message variants to mutate state.

### `src/panels/mod.rs`

```rust
pub mod sidebar;
pub mod detail;
// kitz:panel-mods
```

A standard Rust module file that re-exports the panel submodules.

### `src/panels/sidebar.rs`

A self-contained panel module. It defines:

- A `PANEL_ID` constant (the static string `"sidebar"`).
- A `PANEL_TITLE` constant (the display title `"Sidebar"`).
- A `SidebarPanel` struct holding the panel's local state.
- A `view` method that renders the panel content into a given area.
- A `key_hints` method returning the panel's key bindings.
- Methods for state mutations (`select_next`, `select_prev`).

Panel modules do not implement the `Application` trait. They are plain structs with methods, called by the central `App`. This keeps each panel focused on its own rendering and state, while `app.rs` owns the wiring.

### `src/panels/detail.rs`

Same structure as `sidebar.rs` but for the detail pane.

### `tests/app_test.rs`

A test file set up for `TestHarness`-based testing. You can simulate key presses and assert on application state without needing a terminal:

```rust
use kitz::prelude::*;

// Module imports for your app (generated by kitz new)

// kitz:tests
```

Run tests with `kitz test` or `cargo test`.

---

## Marker Comments

Marker comments are the mechanism that makes `kitz generate` work. They are single-line comments of the form `// kitz:<name>` placed at specific locations in your source files:

| Marker | File | Purpose |
|--------|------|---------|
| `// kitz:messages` | `src/messages.rs` | Insert new message variants |
| `// kitz:update` | `src/app.rs` | Insert new match arms in `update()` |
| `// kitz:app-fields` | `src/app.rs` | Insert new fields on the `App` struct |
| `// kitz:app-init` | `src/app.rs` | Insert field initializers in `App::new()` |
| `// kitz:layout` | `src/app.rs` | Insert new panels into the layout |
| `// kitz:panel-title` | `src/app.rs` | Insert new match arms in `panel_title()` |
| `// kitz:panel-view` | `src/app.rs` | Insert new match arms in `panel_view()` |
| `// kitz:panel-hints` | `src/app.rs` | Insert new match arms in `panel_key_hints()` |
| `// kitz:panel-keys` | `src/app.rs` | Insert new match arms in `panel_handle_key()` |
| `// kitz:panel-mods` | `src/panels/mod.rs` | Insert new `pub mod` declarations |
| `// kitz:tests` | `tests/app_test.rs` | Insert new test functions |

When you run `kitz generate panel stats`, the CLI:

1. Creates `src/panels/stats.rs` with a `StatsPanel` struct.
2. Adds `pub mod stats;` above `// kitz:panel-mods` in `src/panels/mod.rs`.
3. Adds a `stats` field above `// kitz:app-fields` in `src/app.rs`.
4. Adds match arms for `"stats"` in every panel method.
5. Adds message variants above `// kitz:messages`.
6. Adds the corresponding `update` match arm.

All insertions happen directly above the marker line. The marker itself is never removed.

### Can I Remove Markers?

Yes. Markers are only used by `kitz generate`. If you do not plan to use code generation, you can delete every marker comment with no effect on compilation or runtime behavior. If you later want to use `kitz generate` again, re-add the markers at the appropriate locations.

### Can I Rearrange Code Around Markers?

Yes. The CLI searches for the marker string in the file. It does not depend on line numbers or surrounding code. As long as the marker comment exists somewhere in the correct file, generation works.

---

## Adding Components Manually

You do not have to use `kitz generate`. To add a new panel by hand:

1. Create `src/panels/my_panel.rs` following the pattern in `sidebar.rs`.
2. Add `pub mod my_panel;` to `src/panels/mod.rs`.
3. Add a field to the `App` struct and initialize it in `App::new()`.
4. Add match arms in `panel_title`, `panel_view`, `panel_key_hints`, and `panel_handle_key`.
5. Add the panel to the `PanelLayout` returned by `panels()`.
6. Add any new message variants to `src/messages.rs` and handle them in `update`.

The structure is explicit. There is no registration system, no trait-based auto-discovery, and no macros. If you can read the match statement, you understand the wiring.

---

## Scaling Up

As your application grows, you may want to:

- **Split `messages.rs` into submodules** ‚Äî one per feature area, re-exported from a top-level `messages/mod.rs`.
- **Add an `overlays/` directory** ‚Äî for custom overlays, following the same pattern as panels.
- **Add a `screens/` directory** ‚Äî for navigation stack screens.
- **Add a `commands/` or `services/` directory** ‚Äî for async operations and business logic that produces messages.

Kitz does not enforce any of this. The generated structure is a starting point, not a cage.
